description: This figure presents the isothermal phase diagram of the Fe-20Mn-Al-C quaternary system at 1100°C, with mass % Al (0 to 14) on the horizontal axis and mass % C (0 to approximately 2.5) on the vertical axis for a fixed 20 mass % Mn content. The diagram delineates four principal phase fields: a large central gamma (fcc austenite) single-phase region, a gamma + kappa two-phase region at higher Al and C contents, a gamma + alpha two-phase region at high Al and low C contents, and an M3C + gamma region at low Al and high C contents. Superimposed dashed contour lines represent estimated iso-density values of the fcc gamma phase, labeled 6.4, 6.6, 6.8, 7.0, 7.2, 7.4, 7.6, and 7.8 in g/cm³. The figure demonstrates that increasing Al strongly reduces alloy density, while the phase boundaries define the compositional window for single-phase gamma austenite with density below 7.0 g/cm³. This diagram provides the thermodynamic basis for designing the low-density, high-specific-strength Fe-Mn-Al-C(-Cr) alloys studied in the paper, linking composition, phase stability, and density.

Figure 2 comprises two composition–property maps, (a) for Fe–20Mn–Al–C and (b) for Fe–20Mn–Al–C–5Cr (mass%), illustrating the dependence of Al and C contents on Vickers hardness, cold-workability, phase constitution and density after annealing at 1100 °C for 15 min followed by water quenching. In both panels, the horizontal and vertical axes are mass% Al and mass% C, respectively; grey solid lines delineate phase boundaries, while black dashed lines mark constant-density contours ranging from 7.0 to 6.4 g cm⁻³. Individual data points report hardness values (e.g., 295–466 Hv) with cold-workability percentages in parentheses (e.g., >90% or 0%). Panel (a) identifies γ, γ+α, γ+κ and α phase fields; panel (b) replaces the α single-phase field with a γ+α+κ three-phase region and markedly expands the γ single-phase domain toward higher Al and C. The maps demonstrate that increasing Al and C raises hardness but reduces density and cold-workability, whereas 5Cr addition stabilizes austenite: for example, Fe–20Mn–11Al–1.8C–5Cr remains single-phase γ with 71% cold-workability and 431 Hv, whereas the Cr-free counterpart falls in the γ+κ field with 0% cold-workability. These results support that Cr is effective in widening the γ-phase window, enabling a simultaneous achievement of low density, high hardness and substantial room-temperature ductility.

Figure 3 presents optical micrographs showing room-temperature microstructures of Fe–Mn–Al–C–(Cr) alloys after annealing at 1 100°C for 15 min followed by water quenching. Subfigure (a) displays a lamellar two-phase microstructure of ferrite (α) and austenite (γ) in the Fe–20Mn–10Al–0.5C alloy. Subfigure (b) shows a single-phase γ microstructure in the Fe–20Mn–11Al–1.8C–5Cr alloy; caption states that Cr addition suppresses κ-carbide, giving high hardness of 431 Hv and good cold-workability of 71%. Subfigure (c) reveals γ, α, and κ-carbide phases in the Fe–20Mn–13Al–1.8C–5Cr alloy, where the presence of κ phase is reported to drastically reduce cold-workability. Subfigure (d) exhibits an equiaxed γ+α two-phase microstructure in the Fe–20Mn–13Al–1C–5Cr alloy. Overall, the figure illustrates the compositional dependence of phase stability—ranging from α+γ duplex to single γ and to κ-bearing microstructures—which governs the trade-off between strength and cold-workability in these low-density alloys.

Figure 4 presents room-temperature tensile stress–strain curves for low-density Fe–Mn–Al–C-based alloys, comparing quaternary Fe–Mn–Al–C alloys in panel (a) against quinary Fe–Mn–Al–C–5Cr alloys in panel (b). In (a), five curves are shown for Fe-20Mn-10Al-1.5C, Fe-20Mn-13Al-0.75C, Fe-20Mn-11Al-1.0C, Fe-20Mn-10Al-1.0C, and Fe-20Mn-10Al-0.5C, demonstrating that increasing Al and C contents generally raise both yield and tensile strengths. In (b), four curves are displayed for Fe-20Mn-11Al-1.8C-5Cr, Fe-20Mn-13Al-1.3C-5Cr, Fe-20Mn-12Al-1.0C-5Cr, and Fe-20Mn-10Al-1.5C-5Cr, showing similar strengthening trends with enhanced strength levels due to Cr addition. The curves for high-Al and high-C compositions exhibit distinct upper yield points followed by nearly constant flow stress, which caption-related context attributes to nano-size κ-carbide precipitation during cooling. These results support the core conclusion that γ(fcc) Fe–20Mn–Al–C(–Cr) alloys achieve an attractive combination of high specific strength and excellent ductility.

Figure 5 presents room-temperature engineering and true stress–strain curves for Fe–20Mn–10Al–5Cr–1.5C (mass%) alloys annealed at 1100 °C for 15 min followed by water quenching (WQ, ~1100 °C/s) and air cooling (AC, ~15 °C/s). Panel (a) shows engineering stress versus strain: the AC specimen exhibits a pronounced upper yield point near 1050 MPa followed by nearly constant flow stress around 1020–1040 MPa and fractures at approximately 40 % strain, whereas the WQ specimen displays a lower yield strength (~750 MPa) but extensive work-hardening, reaching ~950 MPa before failure at ~65 % strain. Panel (b) presents the corresponding true stress–true strain curves, where the WQ specimen shows almost linear work-hardening, while the AC specimen exhibits near-zero work-hardening below ~5 % true strain followed by gradual hardening. The marked contrast indicates that slower cooling (AC) promotes nano-sized κ-carbide precipitation, raising yield strength but reducing ductility and work-hardening capacity compared with rapid quenching (WQ), supporting the precipitation-hardening mechanism in these low-density Fe–Mn–Al–C-based alloys.

Figure 6 presents transmission electron microscopy characterization of Fe–20Mn–10Al–1.5C–5Cr (mass%) alloy comparing water-quenched and air-cooled conditions. Subfigures (a) and (b) are selected-area electron diffraction patterns: (a) from the water-quenched specimen shows fundamental fcc spots (000, 200, 020) together with weak superlattice reflections such as 100, while (b) from the air-cooled specimen exhibits stronger superlattice reflections, indicating enhanced ordering associated with κ-carbide. Subfigures (c) and (d) are dark-field images taken with the (100)κ superlattice reflection using a 20 nm scale bar. The water-quenched specimen (c) shows only very faint, finely dispersed contrast, confirming minimal nano-precipitate formation, whereas the air-cooled specimen (d) displays distinct cuboidal nano-precipitates aligned along <001> directions. These results demonstrate that slower air cooling promotes pronounced precipitation of nano-size κ-carbide with perovskite structure in the γ matrix, whereas water quenching suppresses it, providing direct microstructural evidence that κ-carbide precipitation is responsible for the high strength and hardness observed in these low-density alloys.

Figure 7 comprises two scatter plots showing tensile strength versus elongation for Fe-Mn-Al-C-based low-density alloys. Subfigure (a) compares water-quenched (WQ, open symbols) and air-cooled (AC, filled symbols) Fe-20Mn-10Al-1.5C-5Cr γ alloys annealed at 1100°C (circles), 1000°C (squares), and 900°C (triangles). Gray arrows indicate that air cooling shifts the data toward higher tensile strength and lower elongation relative to water quenching, while decreasing the annealing temperature from 1100°C to 900°C further reduces both strength and ductility. Subfigure (b) presents as-quenched and cold-rolled Fe-20Mn-Al-C(-5Cr) alloys of varying compositions (Fe20Mn10Al1.2C, Fe20Mn10Al1.5C, Fe20Mn10Al1.5C5Cr, Fe20Mn11Al1.8C5Cr, and Fe20Mn13Al1.0C5Cr), grouped by ellipses into three processing states: as-quenched (moderate strength, high elongation up to ~70%), 30% cold-rolled (elevated strength ~1100–1500 MPa, reduced elongation), and >90% cold-rolled (ultra-high strength ~1800–2100 MPa with minimal elongation). The gray arrows trace the drastic strength gain and ductility loss upon cold rolling. Collectively, the figure demonstrates a clear strength-elongation trade-off governed by cooling rate and cold deformation; caption reports that heavily cold-rolled alloys achieve specific strengths exceeding 300 MPa·cm³/g while 30% cold-rolled Fe-20Mn-11Al-1.8C-5Cr exceeds 230 MPa·cm³/g with over 10% elongation, supporting the development of high-specific-strength low-density steels.

Fig. 8 plots specific strength (MPa·cm³/g) versus tensile elongation (%) for the present Fe–20Mn–Al–C(–Cr) alloys alongside conventional steel grades. The scatter diagram includes data series denoted by distinct symbols: solid circles for the present data, open diamonds for austenitic steels, crosses for ferritic steels, plus signs for martensitic steels, asterisks for precipitation-hardened steels, open triangles for dual-phase steel, open squares for TRIP, open circles for TWIP, and another open-diamond variant for super-toughness steels. A dashed curve marks the approximate upper limit of present conventional steels. The present alloy points cluster above this dashed boundary, demonstrating that the low-density Fe–Mn–Al–C system achieves a superior specific-strength-to-ductility combination compared with traditional austenitic, ferritic, martensitic, DP, TRIP and TWIP steels. Notably, the Fe–20Mn–11Al–1.8C–5Cr alloy (density 6.51 g/cm³) reportedly exceeds 180 MPa·cm³/g in specific strength at approximately 40% elongation. The figure substantiates the paper’s central claim that these κ-carbide-strengthened, low-density steels outperform conventional steels in specific strength while retaining good ductility.
